overview: ACLs (Access Control Lists) filter packets on Cisco devices. Standard ACLs match the source IP only; Extended ACLs match protocol, source/destination IP, and ports.
definitions: ACE (Access Control Entry): one line in an ACL.
Wildcard Mask: '0' = the bit must match, '1' (or '255' in octet form) = ignore the bit.
Standard ACL: matches the source IP only.
Extended ACL: full 5-tuple-style match (protocol / source / destination / port).
core_concepts: First-Match Logic: the router evaluates ACEs top-down and stops at the first matching entry.
Implicit Deny: an invisible 'deny all' sits at the end of every ACL.
Placement rule: place a Standard ACL near the destination; place an Extended ACL near the source.

Named ACLs: allow individual line insertion/deletion, with sequence numbers defaulting to increments of 10. Numbered (old-style) ACLs have no sequence numbers, so editing usually means deleting and rewriting the whole list.
Rule ordering: specific statements must come before general ones — a broad permit placed above a specific deny makes the deny never trigger.
Difficulty split: Standard ACLs are Medium difficulty; Extended ACLs (protocol/port matching and wildcard math) are Hard.
protocol_operation: Packet enters an interface → the inbound ACL is checked (if one is applied).
The packet is compared sequentially against the ACEs.
The first match triggers permit or deny and the search stops.
If permitted, the routing decision is made → the outbound ACL (if any) is checked before egress.
packet_flow: Ingress -> inbound ACL match (sequential, first-match) -> Permit/Deny -> (if permit) routing lookup -> outbound ACL check -> forward or drop. The implicit deny at the end drops anything not explicitly permitted.
header_fields: Standard ACLs match on the IP header source address only.
Extended ACLs additionally match the IP header destination address and the transport header (protocol and port numbers).
Port keywords map to well-known ports: www=80, domain=53, bootps=67.
default_values: ACE sequence numbers increment by 10 by default.
port_numbers: Table 3-3 keywords: www=80, domain=53, bootps=67 (plus the other well-known ports from Chapter 1).
protocol_numbers: ACLs reference protocol numbers when matching by protocol: TCP=6, UDP=17, and ICMP.

config_commands.purpose: Create a Named ACL (global config), enabling per-line editing.
Apply an ACL to an interface in a direction.
Apply an ACL to VTY lines (remote-access restriction).
Delete a single line by its sequence number (in named-ACL mode).

verification_commands.purpose: ACL lines plus per-line match counters.
Shows the applied ACL and its direction.

show_commands.purpose: Read ACL lines and match counters to confirm traffic is hitting the expected rule.
Read a specific named ACL and its per-line match counts.
Confirm which ACL is applied and in which direction.

cli_examples.title: Reading show ip access-lists (Standard)
cli_examples.code:
Standard IP access list 1
  10 permit 10.1.1.1 (107 matches)
  20 deny 10.1.1.0, wildcard bits 0.0.0.255 (4 matches)
cli_examples.explanation: Line 10 matched 107 packets; the match counter confirms whether traffic is actually hitting the expected rule.

cli_examples.title: Reading show access-lists for a named ACL
cli_examples.code:
R1# show access-lists FINANCE-TO-SERVER
10 permit tcp 192.168.20.0 0.0.0.255 host 192.168.30.5 eq 443 (142 matches)
20 deny ip any host 192.168.30.5 (37 matches)
30 permit ip any any (2003 matches)
cli_examples.explanation: 37 blocked attempts on line 20 confirm active attempts to reach the server on a disallowed protocol, and that the rule is working correctly.

config_examples.title: Block Telnet (Extended, named)
config_examples.code:
ip access-list extended BLOCK_TELNET
 deny tcp any any eq 23
 permit ip any any
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip access-group BLOCK_TELNET in

config_examples.title: Restrict Finance to HTTPS-only access to a server (Extended, placed near source)
config_examples.code:
ip access-list extended FINANCE-TO-SERVER
 permit tcp 192.168.20.0 0.0.0.255 host 192.168.30.5 eq 443
 deny ip any host 192.168.30.5
 permit ip any any
!
interface GigabitEthernet0/1
 ip access-group FINANCE-TO-SERVER in
config_examples.explanation: Extended ACL placed close to the source (Finance subnet) — matches the best-placement rule exactly.

config_examples.title: Editing a Named ACL in place
config_examples.code:
ip access-list extended FINANCE-TO-SERVER
 no 20                                                          ! remove line 20 only
 15 permit tcp 192.168.20.0 0.0.0.255 host 192.168.30.5 eq 22   ! insert a new line at sequence 15
config_examples.explanation: Named ACLs support per-line edits via sequence numbers; numbered (old-style) ACLs have no sequence numbers and usually require rewriting the whole list.

config_examples.title: Rule ordering — specific before general (worked)
config_examples.code:
! WRONG — general rule first, so the specific deny never triggers
10 permit ip 192.168.10.0 0.0.0.255 any
20 deny ip host 192.168.10.5 any
!
! CORRECT — specific rule before general
10 deny ip host 192.168.10.5 any
20 permit ip 192.168.10.0 0.0.0.255 any
config_examples.explanation: Goal: block one host (192.168.10.5) from reaching a server while allowing the rest of the subnet. First-match logic means the specific deny must precede the broad permit.

config_examples.title: Wildcard mask worked examples
config_examples.explanation: Subtract each subnet-mask byte from 255 to get the wildcard. Writing the subnet mask instead of the wildcard in an ACL command produces the exact opposite of the intended result — the most dangerous common mistake.

troubleshooting.symptom: A broad 'permit any' placed before a specific 'deny' — the deny never triggers.
troubleshooting.cause: First-match logic stops at the earlier, broader permit.
troubleshooting.fix: Reorder so specific statements (e.g. deny host X) come before general ones (e.g. permit subnet).

troubleshooting.symptom: The ACL has no effect, or it blocks the wrong traffic.
troubleshooting.cause: Wrong interface, or wrong direction (in/out), applied.
troubleshooting.fix: Verify with show ip interface; apply the ACL to the correct interface and direction.

troubleshooting.symptom: A source is blocked from ALL destinations, not just the intended one.
troubleshooting.cause: A Standard ACL was placed near the source (it can only match source IP).
troubleshooting.fix: Place Standard ACLs near the destination; use an Extended ACL near the source when destination-specific filtering is needed.

troubleshooting.symptom: Unsure which rule the traffic is actually hitting.
troubleshooting.fix: Add the 'log' keyword to an ACE, and read match counters via show ip access-lists.

troubleshooting.symptom: A router's own self-originated traffic is not being filtered by an outbound ACL.
troubleshooting.cause: Routers do NOT filter self-originated traffic with outbound ACLs.
troubleshooting.fix: Filter such traffic at the receiving device or via other controls — don't rely on an outbound ACL on the originating router.

troubleshooting.symptom: An OSPF (or other routing-protocol) neighbor relationship breaks after applying an inbound ACL.
troubleshooting.cause: The inbound ACL silently drops routing-protocol hello packets because they aren't explicitly permitted.
troubleshooting.fix: Add an explicit permit for the routing protocol (e.g. OSPF) to the inbound ACL; check show ip access-lists match counters.

troubleshooting.symptom: The ACL produces the exact opposite of the intended result.
troubleshooting.cause: The regular subnet mask was written where a wildcard mask was required.
troubleshooting.fix: Convert to a wildcard (255 minus each subnet-mask byte): e.g. /26 -> 0.0.0.63, not 255.255.255.192.

best_practices: Put specific statements at the top of the ACL.
Use 'remark' to document the intent of each ACL.
Disable the ACL on the interface before making major edits.
security_notes: Implicit deny means an ACL containing only 'deny' statements blocks ALL traffic — a common accidental lockout.
interview_questions: Why use named vs. numbered ACLs?
What does the 'established' keyword do in extended ACLs?
How do you delete one line from a numbered ACL without deleting it all?
Where should a standard ACL be placed?
exam_traps: Matching a single host requires the 'host' keyword or a 0.0.0.0 wildcard.
Writing a subnet mask where a wildcard mask is required — produces the opposite result.
enterprise_notes: In WAN scenarios (OSPF over MPLS + NAT + ACL), an inbound ACL blocking OSPF hello packets is a common root cause of neighbor-relationship failure — check show ip access-lists match counters as part of the troubleshooting chain.
